articles.headline: New-York Petitions to the Legislature (repeal, jury trial, suffrage)
articles.summary: Report on three petitions from New York City's Black community to the state Legislature, forwarded the prior week to Albany: (1) repeal of the state law authorizing slaveholding -- 276 petitioners (605 men, 271 women; the paper's tallies are inconsistent but ~276 names, 25 feet long); (2) jury trial for persons of color claimed as fugitive slaves -- 761 names (189 males, 272 females; sic, totals as printed), 23 feet long; (3) an alteration of the state Constitution to extend suffrage to Black men on equal terms with whites -- 620 men's names, 20 feet long, more than half in the signers' own handwriting. Notes the signature of Independence Roberts, born July 4, 1776 in Philadelphia, and of Pompey Wilson, born 1710 in New York City. Contrasts with an 1821 remonstrance against disfranchisement that drew only ~50 names. A companion Brooklyn jury-trial petition (202 names: 110 men, 92 women) was forwarded via Richard W. Thorne, Esq., Member of Assembly from Brooklyn; the New York petitions were sent via Mr. Zabriskie, Member of Assembly, accompanied by a supporting statement from Samuel E. Cornish as Chairman of the Delegation.

articles.headline: Reception of the Petitions in the Assembly (Albany, March 6, 1837)
articles.summary: Albany Daily Advertiser account of the petitions' presentation: Mr. Zabriskie presented the New York/Brooklyn petitions and spoke in favor of emancipation (though opposed to 'the doctrines of the Abolitionists'). Additional petitions were read from New Haven/Oswego Co., Arcade/Genesee Co., and 21 colored persons of Albany. Debate among Messrs. C.O. Shepherd, King, T.W. Tucker, and Bradish over withdrawing/denying the Albany petition; the previous question carried 47-25, and the motion to deny the Albany petition's prayer carried 71-24 (nays listed: Andrew, Bradish, Case, Franklin, Hammond, Hurd, Labagh, Patterson, Plumb, Riley?, Russell, Seward, C.O. Shepherd, Sibly, Smead, Townsend, ?, Thows, Walker).

articles.headline: The Brig Rising States -- Black-owned, Black-crewed whaler
articles.summary: The brig Rising States, owned by a company of colored men in New Bedford and manned entirely by a Black crew of fifteen officers and men, cost $5,500 outfitted and sailed from New Bedford for a twelve-month South Atlantic whaling voyage. Officers: Richard Johnson (Agent), Edward J. Pompey (Master), William Cutler (Mate), William Harefoot (2d Mate). Accompanying commentary (reprinted from an abolitionist paper) contrasts the press's likely silence/sneering over this Black-owned brig with the fanfare that would greet a similar achievement credited to the Liberian colonization project.

articles.headline: Eulogy for the late William Hamilton, Sen., to be delivered by Henry Sipkins
articles.summary: Notice that an Eulogy on the life and character of the late William Hamilton Sen. will be delivered by Mr. Henry Sipkins on Wednesday evening, March 22, 1837, at Zion's Church, New York.

articles.headline: Directory of Colored Churches and Sabbath Schools in the City of New York
articles.summary: Full roster of New York City's Black churches, ministers, and colored public schools, published to inform readers of available places of worship and instruction. Churches/ministers include: St. Philip's Church (Episcopal), Centre St. between Anthony and Leonard -- Rev. Peter Williams, Rector; Zion's Church, corner William and Frankfort -- Rev. Christopher Rush, Pastor; Zion Church (Methodist), corner Church and Chapel Streets -- Rev. James Dorsett?, Elder; Abyssinian Baptist Church -- Rev. A.S. Raymond; Bethel Church (African Methodist Episcopal), between Avenue C and D -- Rev. Wm. Cornish, Elder; Asbury Church (Methodist), Mulberry St. -- Rev. Timothy Eato, Elder; a Harlem Methodist Church (Zion's Connexion, Sixth Ward). Colored public schools/teachers noted: Miss Ellen Richards (principal, female department); John Peterson (teacher, male department, Bethel-adjacent school); Fanny Tompkins (female department teacher); Miss L. Merritt (principal, another ward school); Miss C. Roe (principal, female department). Mutual Relief Hall listed as a meeting place, corner of Orange and (street illegible).

articles.headline: Colored People of Troy -- Fourth Anniversary of the Female Benevolent Society
articles.author: John J. Miller
articles.summary: Report by John J. Miller (United Sons and Daughters of Zion's Benevolent Society) on the fourth anniversary of Troy's Female Benevolent Society, held Feb. 13, 1837 in the colored people's new church on Liberty St. Rev. Mr. Vandevere (Elder, Methodist Church) and Mr. Harris (a young Black man, junior class at Vermont University) addressed the Society from the pulpit. Society officers (President, Vice-President, Directresses) and the Secretary sat on a platform; members wore a plain white scarf. Treasurer's report: $228.29 on deposit at the Troy Savings Bank plus $8 on hand for relief, total fund of $236.39 over four years; $89 spent on sick members and $7.86 on funeral expenses that year. A companion men's/mixed benevolent group, the 'United Sons and Daughters of Zion's Benevolent Society,' was formed Jan. 8, 1835, held its second anniversary Nov. 25 (1836), had $112 at interest, spent $15 relieving the poor, and had grown to 48 members.

articles.headline: Colored People of Troy -- Union Protracted Meeting, six evenings
articles.author: John J. Miller
articles.summary: Six-evening 'union protracted meeting' among Troy's Black Methodist and Presbyterian congregations (proposed by Miller), addressing education, temperance/total abstinence, Sabbath observance, moral reform, and a temperance/female temperance society. Speakers named: Brother Wells (preaching for the Presbyterian church), Vandevere, Harris, and Butler. At the education meeting, $22 was subscribed for the day school ($15 paid on the spot), on top of $78 the colored community had already paid its teacher that winter. At the temperance meeting sixty signed a total-abstinence pledge; a new male total-abstinence society of 26 members was formed with officers Daniel Vandevere (President), Alex Thury (Vice-President), and Clarence Silden (Secretary). A Female Temperance Society was also organized (68 members, making 94 total across both new societies).

articles.headline: New Agents and Donations acknowledged
articles.summary: Rev. Charles B. Ray of New York named the paper's first General Agent, authorized to collect subscriptions and donations nationally. New out-of-city agents named: Mr. Sidney (Hartford, CT), Amos G. Beman (Hartford), Amos Freeman (New Brunswick, NJ), and Jonathan [Mingo] (cut off in this issue, confirmed April 8). A $10 donation from Hon. William Jay acknowledged. Endorsement of the paper reprinted with an expanded signer list: Arthur Tappan, Joshua Leavitt, Lewis Tappan, Simeon S. Jocelyn, R.G. Williams, Theodore S. Wright Jr., Gerrit Smith, Peter Williams, A.A. Phelps, Theo. S. Wright.

articles.headline: New Jersey's new certificate law targeting free Black residents and travelers
articles.summary: Editorial condemning a New Jersey legislative act (reprinted from the Newark Daily Advertiser) requiring any free Black person from another state entering New Jersey to carry a certificate of freedom from two justices of the peace of their home state (county-clerk certified), to be deposited with a New Jersey county clerk within ten days on penalty of a $10 fine per 20 days of neglect; residents already in the state faced similar registration requirements, with fines also for New Jersey residents who harbored or employed uncertified Black people. The editor argues the law is unconstitutional (citing Art. 4, Sec. 2, U.S. Constitution on the privileges/immunities of citizens) and urges free Black New Yorkers to disregard it if they choose to visit New Jersey, and asks the Newark Daily Advertiser to publish the names of the legislators responsible 'to hand them down...to posterity.'

articles.headline: William Rich of Troy sends 13 new subscribers; New Jersey and other new agents
articles.summary: Letter and remittance from William Rich (agent, Troy) enclosing 13 new subscribers and payment, praised warmly by the editor as a model of community mobilization. Additional new agents acknowledged: Sidney W. Rockwell (Hartford, CT), Amos G. Beman (Hartford), Amos Freeman (New Brunswick, NJ), Jonathan Mingo (Flushing, Long Island). Donations acknowledged: $19 from William Rich (Troy), $10 from Mr. Jennings (Boston), 50 cts. from Mr. Freeman (New Brunswick), $1.50 from Chas. Payne (Newburgh).

articles.headline: The fugitive George Thompson, alias 'Ben' -- extradition case
articles.summary: Case of George Thompson, alias 'Ben,' decided by Judge Irving, who ruled that Governor Marcy's warrant was sufficient to hold the prisoner despite defense counsel (Messrs. Dresser and Sedgwick) arguing the warrant did not establish jurisdiction; the prosecution was represented by Messrs. Phoenix and Sandford. 'Ben' was placed aboard a vessel the following Sunday morning to be returned south; his treatment while awaiting transport is described as harsh. Gen. Wa[shington?], Sheriff of the county, was said to have some claim to the prisoner's services.

articles.headline: Fourth Anniversary of the American Anti-Slavery Society announced
articles.author: Lewis Tappan; Samuel E. Cornish; Simeon S. Jocelyn (Committee)
articles.summary: Notice that the American Anti-Slavery Society's Fourth Anniversary would be held in New York City on (date partly illegible) May 1837 at the Broadway Tabernacle, with a delegates' meeting the preceding Monday at the Society's rooms, 143 Nassau St. (opposite the Brick Church). Signed by the Committee: Lewis Tappan, Samuel E. Cornish, Simeon S. Jocelyn.

articles.headline: New-York Committee of Vigilance -- first annual report published
articles.summary: Notice of the publication of the New-York Committee of Vigilance's first annual report (the Committee, formed the previous year to protect Black New Yorkers from kidnapping and other oppressions by courts, civil officers, and 'land pirates'), described as containing facts and information that should be known to every person in the nation.

events.name: Adjourned Meeting of the People of Color, New York City (petitions to Albany)
events.location: Phenix Hall, 165 Chapel Street, New York City
events.description: Meeting resolving that, due to the difficulty and expense of spring travel, no delegation would be sent to Albany with the petitions; instead the petitions were sealed and sent by mail via Mr. Zabriskie, Assemblyman, with a supporting statement of facts prepared and signed by Samuel E. Cornish as Chairman of the Delegation. Also reports the Mutual Relief Society's regular monthly meeting (Monday March 13) with P.A. Bell as Secretary, and an extra meeting of the New-York Union Society at Philomathean Hall (also March 13) called by Secretary W.P. Johnson.
attendees: Chairman of the Delegation; drafted supporting statement to Mr. Zabriskie

events.name: New-York Committee of Vigilance meeting re: Thomas Bryan, Thomas Oliver, John Robinson Welch
events.location: Phenix Hall, Chapel Street (or West Broadway), New York City
events.description: Important meeting of the New-York Committee of Vigilance's Executive Committee called for Monday, March 13, 1837, to address the cases of Thomas Bryan (a free Black boy from New York City imprisoned at Vicksburg, Mississippi to be sold for his jail fees) and Thomas Oliver (recently sold as a slave in New Orleans), and to gather information on the disappearance of John Robinson Welch, a mulatto boy aged 8-9 last seen Feb. 23. Notice signed by D. Ruggles, Secretary, New-York Committee of Vigilance.
attendees: free Black boy imprisoned at Vicksburg, MS, subject of the meeting
sold as a slave in New Orleans, subject of the meeting
missing mulatto boy, subject of the meeting

events.name: Adjourned Meeting of the People of Color, New York City (Mutual Relief Society notice)
events.description: Resolution approving and recommending 'The Colored American' newspaper, moved by Thomas L. Jennings and seconded by several persons; Wm. P. Johnson recorded as Secretary. Also announces the March 22 Eulogy for William Hamilton, Sen. by Henry Sipkins at Zion's Church.
attendees: moved the resolution recommending the paper

events.name: Troy Female Benevolent Society Fourth Anniversary
events.location: colored people's new church, Liberty Street, Troy, NY
events.description: Fourth anniversary celebration of the Female Benevolent Society of Troy's Black community. Rev. Mr. Vandevere (Methodist Elder) and Mr. Harris (Vermont University student) addressed the gathering; compositions by younger members were read, including a letter from a 14-year-old member attending a Philadelphia boarding school. Treasury held $236.39 after four years of operation.
attendees: Elder, Methodist Church; addressed the Society
junior, Vermont University; addressed the Society
correspondent reporting the anniversary; officer, United Sons and Daughters of Zion's Benevolent Society

events.name: Troy Union Protracted Meeting (education, temperance, Sabbath, moral reform)
events.location: Methodist and Presbyterian churches, Troy, NY
events.description: Six consecutive evening meetings (beginning Wed., March 1, 1837) uniting Troy's Black Methodist and Presbyterian congregations around education, total abstinence, Sabbath observance, and moral reform. Resulted in $22 raised for the day school, 60 signatures to a total-abstinence pledge, and formation of both a men's Total Abstinence Society (26 members) and a Female Temperance Society (68 members).
attendees: President, new Total Abstinence Male Society
Vice-President, new Total Abstinence Male Society
Secretary, new Total Abstinence Male Society
speaker; preaching for the Presbyterian church

events.name: Adjourned Meeting of the People of Color, New York City (resolution recommending the paper)
events.description: Resolution approving and recommending 'The Colored American,' moved by Thomas L. Jennings and seconded by several persons speaking at once. Thomas Van Rensselaer recorded as Chairman and Wm. P. Johnson as Secretary of the meeting.

events.name: George Thompson ('Ben') fugitive extradition ruling
events.description: Judge Irving ruled that Governor Marcy's warrant sufficed to hold George Thompson, alias 'Ben,' for return south as a fugitive slave, over defense objections from counsel Dresser and Sedgwick that the warrant did not establish jurisdiction; prosecuted by Phoenix and Sandford. Thompson was placed on a southbound vessel the following Sunday.
attendees: fugitive, alias 'Ben'; subject of the ruling

names: agent, Hartford, CT (surname continued/corrected in April 8 issue as Sidney W. Rockwell)
agents.note: New out-of-city agents announced alongside Rev. Charles B. Ray's appointment as General Agent.
Confirms and completes the agent roster begun in the April 1 issue.
